I was hoping we could explore one option together before we decide.
Victor, you have fifteen minutes. Give me the quarter, and then the ask.

Let me headline it: revenue is up nineteen percent. The market had been sliding while we rebuilt the pipeline, then in March we launched the new route, and so we go live, and within a week the orders double.
Strong story. So what is the value proposition for next year?
If we funded two more regions, we would triple the reach within a year. I would frame it as an investment, not a cost, and I think it would win quick buy-in from operations.
Two regions is a big number. The CFO will push back on the budget.
That is why I would propose a pilot. If you approve one region first, we will share milestones in ninety days, and unless the numbers hold, we pause. That keeps the trade-off small.
Hm. Let me think about the timing.
You have gone quiet, so you might be weighing the risk. I do not want to push, I was hoping we could explore the pilot timing together before you decide.
I appreciate that. Run the pilot on one region. If the milestones hold at ninety days, we will talk about the second.
If the value proposition is clear, we would win buy-in fast.
I read the room, acknowledged her doubt, then offered a pilot.
She is gone quiet, so she might be weighing the risk.

Okay, the quarterly stakeholder meeting is in an hour, and this one needs every skill from the last few weeks. I open by telling the story: the market had been sliding while we rebuilt the pipeline, then in March we launched the new route, and so we go live, and the orders double. That is my headline and my turning point. Then comes the pitch. If we funded two more regions, we would triple the reach within a year, so I frame it as an investment, not a cost. They will push back on budget, so I prepare the negotiation: if they approve the pilot, we will share results in ninety days, and unless the numbers hold, we pause. I also have to read the room. The CFO went quiet last time, which usually means she has doubts, so she might be weighing the risk rather than rejecting it. And if it gets tense, I stay tentative: I was hoping we could explore one option together, rather than pushing. Story, pitch, deal, read, care. Signpost every move and land one takeaway. Right, let me run it once more.

Hi Victor, it is Diana. Quick note before the stakeholder meeting so we are aligned. Three things the room will be watching for. First, structure. Lead with the story and the headline, signpost as you go, and close on one clear takeaway, because a stakeholder who knows the map will follow you. Second, the ask. When you pitch the two new regions, frame it as an investment and be ready to negotiate the conditions. If the board approves a pilot, we will want milestones, so come with a plan: if the numbers hold, we scale; if they do not, we pause. That kind of conditional offer builds trust. Third, the room itself. Read the signals. If someone goes quiet, they may be weighing the risk, not rejecting it, so invite them in gently rather than pushing. And if it turns tense, stay tentative and curious. You have done every piece of this in the last five classes, Victor. Today you just put them together. Go run a clean meeting.
